nativeRange.habitat: Shallow margins of ponds, lakes, marshes, and slow-moving streams; tolerates brackish coastal wetlands at the low end of its salinity range

avoidWith: vigorously digging fish (large loaches, geophagine cichlids)

summary: Dwarf Hairgrass (Eleocharis parvula) is one of the most popular foreground carpeting plants in freshwater aquascaping, forming a grass-like lawn across the substrate via spreading underground runners. High light and CO2 injection are strongly recommended for a tight, low carpet; under lower light it tends to grow tall and sparse.
careNotes: Dwarf Hairgrass is one of the most widely recognised foreground carpeting plants in the freshwater hobby, prized for the dense, grass-like lawn it can form when given suitable conditions. The plant sold under this name is most commonly Eleocharis parvula, though it is frequently confused in the trade with the closely related E. acicularis; E. parvula stays shorter (generally up to around 10–12 cm), while E. acicularis tends slightly taller (around 10–20 cm). The cultivar names seen in shops — 'Mini', 'Belem', and others — are selections within E. parvula or its close relatives. Lighting is the single most important parameter: under medium-to-high light (PAR ~50+ at substrate) the plant carpets low and tight; under low light it tends to grow vertical and sparse, often failing as a carpet altogether. CO2 injection is technically optional but strongly recommended for the dense, low carpet look most aquascapers want — without it the plant generally survives but will not form a true tight lawn. Dwarf Hairgrass is a moderate root feeder, and a nutrient-rich substrate (aquasoil) or an inert substrate supplemented with root tabs gives substantially better results than plain gravel. The comfortable temperature range in the hobby runs from 22–28 °C, with reported tolerance down to around 18 °C. Propagation happens through substrate runners that spread laterally from the parent rosette; trimming the carpet short after it establishes tends to encourage denser horizontal growth rather than upward reach. New plantings are best separated into small clumps of around 3–5 blades each and planted on a grid roughly 2–3 cm apart for fastest fill-in. Algae on the blades is a common problem during the early weeks; maintaining CO2 and keeping nitrate and phosphate levels lean during establishment generally helps.
breedingNotes: Vegetative propagation only in aquarium settings: spreads by horizontal underground runners (rhizomatous stolons) that emerge as new rosettes a few centimeters from the parent. Established carpets can be lifted, divided into small clumps, and replanted to expand coverage or restart a tank. Flowering and seed production are possible under emersed conditions but essentially never occur in submerged aquarium use.

primary.name: Modern Aquarium
primary.notes: Primary care reference. Confirmed temperature range 72–82 °F (22–28 °C), medium-to-high lighting (stronger light keeps carpet low and dense), CO2 strongly responsive but not strictly required, moderate growth rate accelerating with CO2/light/substrate, foreground placement, runner propagation through substrate, nutrient-rich substrate preferred (Fluval Stratum / root tabs), moderate care level — easy to keep alive, harder to achieve a true tight carpet.

additional.name: Aquarium Co-Op
additional.notes: Cross-check: medium-to-high lighting required, runner-based propagation forms a lush carpet, moderately easy care level. Page did not specify CO2/temp/pH/hardness numerics; used Modern Aquarium for those.
